summary: Multi-agent framework (Instrument → Analyze → Repair) that captures runtime traces, applies a Historical Lesson Learning Mechanism (HLLM) to distill insights from prior failed repair attempts, and injects them into subsequent repair prompts. Achieved 34.43% improvement in Pass@1 bug-fix accuracy over best non-HLLM baseline.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — maps directly to the AddressingReview remediation loop; HLLM pattern slots into the existing AgentSkill store and promptRenderer without new stages.

source: Dynamic Model Routing and Cascading for Efficient LLM Inference — Survey (arxiv:2603.04445)
summary: Survey establishing that complexity-based model routing achieves 60–75% cost reduction with comparable output quality; real-world legal agent achieved 75% cost reduction by routing simple steps to mid-tier models and reserving frontier models only for final judgment.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentRunner has no model-tier selection; hardcoded runtime per stage pays flagship pricing for all tasks regardless of complexity.

summary: 630-line Python script that runs experiments in a tight commit-or-rollback loop: agent modifies training code, runs time-boxed experiment, keeps change if metric improves, rolls back otherwise. 21k+ GitHub stars; ran 700 experiments in 2 days autonomously.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — inspired the AIBlocked auto-recovery issue (HID-86); the commit-or-rollback pattern maps to AgentForge's need for automatic retry from AIBlocked without human intervention.

source: agentmemory by rohitg00 — Persistent Cross-Run Memory for Coding Agents
summary: MCP-compatible persistent memory layer for coding agents (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, etc.) that silently captures execution history, compresses it into searchable memory (BM25 + embedding + reranker), and injects relevant context at the start of each new session. v0.9.22; 100% top-5 hit rate on benchmark.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — co-inspired HID-86 (negative-example injection into recovery prompts); also relevant to limitation #8 (no agent memory across runs). Full episodic memory injection is a future candidate.

source: Sherlock — Reliable and Efficient Agentic Workflow Execution (arxiv:2511.00330)
summary: Microsoft Research system that uses fault-injection-based vulnerability analysis to place verifiers at error-prone nodes, then speculatively executes downstream tasks while verification runs in background; rolls back on failure. 18.3% accuracy gain, 48.7% latency reduction.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — speculative stage prep during policy checks could reduce latency; selective verification maps loosely to policy engine gate placement. Architecturally more complex than the value justifies for a 1-2 sprint issue.

source: Speculative Actions — A Lossless Framework for Faster Agentic Systems (arxiv:2510.04371)
summary: Uses faster draft models to predict likely future actions and execute them in parallel; commits when predictions match the ground-truth executor. Up to 55% next-action accuracy and 20% latency reduction across gaming, e-commerce, web search benchmarks.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — AgentForge is single-agent-per-stage sequential; speculative parallel stage prep is architecturally feasible but requires significant ProcessRunner changes and the sequential state machine assumption would need revisiting.

summary: Framework for automatically evolving agentic workflows using TextGrad, AFlow, and MIPRO optimization algorithms; constructs multi-agent workflows from a single prompt and iteratively refines prompts, tool configs, and workflow topologies.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — EvoAgentX is a full framework replacement; its optimization algorithms could eventually inspire prompt auto-tuning in AgentForge but require a significant rewrite to adapt to the deterministic state machine model.

source: NVIDIA ProRL Agent — Rollout-as-a-Service for RL Training (arxiv:2603.18815)
summary: Decoupled RL training infrastructure where an HTTP service manages the full agent rollout lifecycle (INIT/RUN/EVAL workers) independently of the GPU-intensive training loop, enabling scalable multi-turn LLM agent RL training.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — ProRL addresses training infrastructure at scale; AgentForge is an orchestration system, not an RL training platform. The decoupled service architecture is interesting but solving a different problem class.

summary: Infrastructure-level dispatch that partitions vLLM fleet into short-context and long-context pools, routing by estimated token budget. 31–42% GPU-hour reduction at fleet scale.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — Fleet-level serving infrastructure; AgentForge calls CLIs (subprocess), not a vLLM serving fleet. The token-budget signal concept is relevant to HID-85 but the full paper is out of scope.

source: Reflection-Driven Control for Trustworthy Code Agents (arxiv:2512.21354)
summary: Pluggable control module that monitors agent reasoning during generation, detects risks, and retrieves repair examples from an evolving reflective memory to inject security constraints into subsequent reasoning steps. Evaluated on 8 security-critical programming tasks.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — The reflective memory retrieval pattern is directly related to HID-84 (historical lesson injection); however the security-focus and in-generation monitoring make it less directly applicable than TraceCoder's HLLM which targets the same failure-learning problem with more direct benchmarks.

summary: Standard Python coding agent framework that works on its own codebase; improved SWE-Bench Verified from 17% to 53% through self-improvement loops with safety constraints.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — The self-improvement loop concept is interesting but SICA modifies its own codebase, which is architecturally orthogonal to AgentForge's orchestration model. Would revisit if self-hosted agent improvement becomes a priority.

source: Runtime Execution Traces Guided APR with Multi-Agent Debate (arxiv:2604.02647)
summary: Multi-agent debate framework guided by runtime execution traces for automated program repair; agents debate repair strategies informed by trace analysis, improving consensus quality.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — The multi-agent debate pattern on top of trace analysis extends TraceCoder's approach; premature for AgentForge which is single-agent-per-stage. Watch for when AddressingReview can support parallel debate agents.
